tweets.text:
I don't net short with leverage. Nothing against people who do it well, and there are some that do and are very talented. It's just not my strength and not my style. If I'm bearish I go to cash, or hedge spot a little bit. You will see me disappear during those times bc I'm just not trading. 

The math is why. Longs can go to infinity, they are uncapped on upside where as shorts are capped. Essentially best case it goes to 0 and you make a 2x. 

SNDK did 60x or so in the past year. 50% fall pays you 50% whether you nailed a parabolic top or shorted something like ETH which has already been cut in half if it fell in half now. The short doesn't care how extended the move was. People who've been trying to call tops with levered shorts, well, it's typically an ego thing. More often than not. it's better to short the thing that has been performing like crap anyways. idk what the obsession  is with shorting strength repeatedly 

Then in equities you have other things that go against you. It's carry. Shorts bleed, you pay the borrow, pay the dividend, fighting stock buybacks, inflation, etc. The timing has to be pretty precise. And then there are naked shorts, where you end up shorting something and end up losing 3-4x the amount you invested. Short MU at $250, it goes to 1k. It happens all the time. 

It is so much better to just have a few good plays, and then every once in awhile you find a high conviction bet to really just push to the max. Even for me, most trades I take are pretty consistent, and once a year I hit a trade that makes up like 70% of my years gains bc I knew it was the one. But that's just my style.

tweets.text:
Americans have rarely been this bullish on equities:

In December, 55% of US consumers expected higher stock prices over the next 12 months, the 3rd highest monthly reading since this question was first asked in 1987.

Since April 2025, bullish sentiment has surged +17 percentage points and is just shy of its all-time high set in December 2024.

By comparison, this metric peaked at ~47% during the 2000 Dot-Com Bubble and at ~51% in 2018.

This is also well above the 49-year average of 36%.

Meanwhile, only 25% of consumers expect lower stock prices over the next year, near the lowest since 2020.

This metric stands below its long-term average of 28% and the March 2025 high of 47%.

Bullish sentiment is incredibly strong.

Inflation is increasingly outpacing American incomes:

Real disposable income fell -1.1% YoY in April, the biggest decline since October 2022.

On an annualized MoM basis, this metric dropped -5.6%, the biggest decline since May 2025.

Furthermore, real personal income excluding government transfer payments fell -0.4%, the 4th monthly decline in 5 months.

This metric captures after-tax income from labor and capital sources while excluding government support such as Social Security and unemployment benefits.

Over the last 6 months, real personal income excluding transfer payments has declined by an annualized -1.8%, the largest drop since 2022.

Historically, such weakness has rarely occurred outside of a recession or an economic slowdown.

Inflation is eroding the purchasing power of most Americans.

World central banks are increasingly moving toward tightening:

Global central banks have cut rates 31 times year-to-date, outpacing 12 hikes conducted over the same period.

Over the last 3 months, there were just ~3 more rate cuts than hikes globally, the smallest gap since 2023.

Developed markets have now implemented 3 more rate hikes than cuts over this period.

At the same time, emerging markets have still seen 6 more rate cuts than hikes.

To put this into perspective, world central banks hiked rates ~90 times more than they cut in a single 3-month period during the peak of the 2022 inflation crisis.

Resurgent inflation points to more rate hikes ahead.

Resurgent inflation points to more rate hikes ahead.

tweets.text:
China's shrinking appetite for oil is balancing global crude markets:

China's crude oil imports are tracking at ~6.6 million barrels per day so far in May, the lowest since 2016/

This comes as oil inbound shipments fell -20% MoM in April, to 9.37 million barrels per day, the lowest since July 2022.

This compares to 11.6 million barrels per day in 2025, when the country was aggressively stockpiling to bolster energy security.

China’s crude oil imports are now expected to average 10.9 million barrels per day in 2026, the lowest average for any year since 2022.

However, China's oil inventories have declined by only ~20 million barrels over the last several weeks, to ~1.23 billion barrels, still +15% above early 2025 levels.

Over the prior 2 years, the country’s stockpiling absorbed much of the world's surplus oil and helped support global prices, but this dynamic has now reversed and is capping any further upside.

China is effectively putting a ceiling on oil price gains despite severe supply shortages.

thread_tweets.text:
Great question... 
1) Use the tools daily on actual problems, not just for simple things that the majority of people are using it for. Trying new tools is very important. 

2) Surround yourself with the people that are interested in building... your peer group is your single biggest leading indicator.

 3) I try to stay as close to the source as possible, founders, researchers, primary papers, etc. 

 4) Having a strong MTP. I look at things as "what's now possible?", and try to spread that vision onto others rather than thinking "what's at risk?... 

Things are moving fast, and truly, it's impossible to stay 100% on top of everything, but doing something and at least staying connected is better than not paying attention.

tweets.text:
Awareness: The Final Frontier

Close your eyes and ask yourself: Where does awareness begin, and where does it end?

A house has boundaries. Your body has boundaries. Countries have boundaries. But can you find the boundary of awareness?

Most people cannot.

Now open your eyes. The world appears. The sky, the trees, your body, other people. All of it appears in awareness.

Every scientific discovery appears in awareness. The stars, the galaxies, the universe itself. You know them only because you are aware of them.

The same is true of God. Whether God is real or not, your experience, belief, or idea of God appears in awareness.

The same is true of the soul. Whether you believe you have one or not, that belief and experience appear in awareness.

Everything you know of the world, science, God, soul, and self appears in awareness.

But if awareness is truly personal, where are its boundaries?

We say “my awareness” and “your awareness,” but ownership requires a boundary.

A house can be yours because it has boundaries. A body can be yours because it has boundaries.

Can you find where your awareness ends and another person’s begins?

Or have you simply assumed the boundary is there because bodies appear separate?

Now look again. You see other people and you see your own body. But both are known in exactly the same way: as appearances in awareness.

What makes one “me” and the other “other”? Is that distinction directly experienced, or is it inferred?

In a dream, there are people, objects, places, and even a dream version of you. Everything appears separate. Yet upon waking, you realize it all appeared within a single field of consciousness.

The separation appeared real. Whether it was ultimately real is another question.

We spend our lives investigating what appears in awareness, but rarely investigate awareness itself.

Can you find anything outside awareness?

Can you find where the world ends and awareness begins?

Can you find where your awareness ends and mine begins?

Or have you simply assumed those boundaries exist?

Perhaps awareness is the final frontier.

Not what appears within it.

But that in which everything appears.

When structural demand collides with stagnant production.

You can’t just flip a switch and grow mine output overnight.

Discoveries are rarer, permitting is harder, grades are falling, and development timelines keep getting longer.

Contrary to what most believe, this is not a speculative bubble.

It is the market adjusting to a new reality of structurally higher resource prices.

Wake me up when production actually starts surging.

anthropic and openai have now each released multi-agent autonomous features over the last week confirming a very strong shift to agentic ai

- codex can now effectively do the job of a 5 person engineering team. it plans, spins up multiple coding flows and just executes. 

- anthropic launched dynamic workflows with opus 4.8 that pretty much does the same thing.

both companies let you do this while… being on your phone. text your agent and it continues working on your desktop at home. 

this shift means we have another series of bottlenecks:

- memory chips and GPUs scarce
- intents. llms need to learn the nuance and context behind the workflows they’re trying to replace.

both startups made it clear enterprise is where the money is so both are starting there.

The idea that there are some things that can happen and DO happen while there are other things that can happen but DON'T happen is very philosophically unappealing. Who or what decides what becomes real or not?

A much more appealing idea is that everything that can happen does happen, and the more ways in which something can happen, the more often it happens. 

That's what a literal interpretation of the mathematics of quantum mechanics says at least.

So whatever photo the AI generates, unless it shows something that is forbidden by the laws of physics, will exist in some emergent branch of the wave function. 

And yes, this actually has some investment implications:
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
I said this to @citrini last night, but in the future, will we really need storage? 

I take a ton of photos of my kids, and they are on my phone and in a cloud. But in the future, won't I just tell a model "generate a photo from my son's 7th birthday" and it'll be just as good?

Here is the relevant section of the report:

The Doomsday Argument

There is a catch, however. Superintelligent AI has the potential to be dangerous, so dangerous in fact that one might wonder if it is just a coincidence that you are reading these words at the dawn of the AI age rather than in the distant future.

To see why this could be concerning, consider the following thought experiment, first suggested by philosopher Nick Bostrom:
Suppose that two indistinguishable urns are placed in front of you. One urn contains ten balls numbered 1 through 10, while the other urn contains one million balls numbered 1 through 1,000,000. Without any further information, the best you can say is that there is a 50-50 chance that one of the two urns contains the smaller number of balls.

Now, suppose you dip your hand in one of the urns and pull out a ball numbered “7.” What are the chances that you drew the ball from the urn with the smaller number of balls? 

One could argue that the odds should remain 50-50 since there is a “7” in both urns. One could argue this, but one would be wrong. A simple application of Bayes’ rule implies that the probability that you drew the “7” from the urn with 10 balls should rise from 50% to 99.999%.1

More generally, if you do not know anything about the size of the distribution from which you are sampling, your best bet would be just to assume you are somewhere in the middle of the distribution. During World War II, the Allies used this method to estimate the total number of tanks that the Germans possessed based on the serial numbers of the tanks’ components. 

Now, let us change the thought experiment a little bit. Suppose you want to evaluate two competing hypotheses: The first hypothesis says only 200 billion people will ever live on Earth, while the second says that 200 quadrillion will live.

If you are alive today, then you are roughly the 100 billionth person to have been born. If 200 quadrillion people will ever be born, you would be among the first 0.00005% to have ever lived. That seems very unlikely. Hence, if you accept the logic from the urn example, you should reject the hypothesis of a very long-lived Earth with overwhelming certainty. 

The reasoning discussed above is popularly known in the philosophy literature as the “Doomsday Argument.” It was first formulated by physicist Brandon Carter and later refined by philosopher John Leslie. 

A Dubious Argument?

People who encounter the Doomsday Argument for the first time usually conclude that it must obviously be flawed. However, as philosopher Nick Bostrom has noted, most of the counterarguments to the Doomsday Argument are themselves flawed. Bostrom’s view is that if the Doomsday Argument is indeed flawed, it is flawed because of deep methodological considerations – such as how to properly define a reference class for all human observers – rather than because of something trivial.

My own view is nuanced. Given that I am alive in 2026, I agree that I should tilt my view towards thinking that only 200 billion people will ever be born, rather than say, 200 quadrillion. However, this should be just one piece of evidence in favor of that conclusion, and certainly not the most important one. 

To see why, consider a slightly different example where you have to estimate how long you personally will live. If you knew absolutely nothing about how long humans lived, then estimating your longevity by simply doubling your current age might be the best that you could do. But realistically, you could obviously do much better than that. You could, for example, look at an actuarial table. You could also refine your estimate by incorporating information about your genetic history and lifestyle choices.

tweets.text:
People ask me why I don't use agent-native products instead of Gmail or WhatsApp. 

The same misconception happened during the mobile era, where we thought we were going to have a mobile-native mailbox or cloud or CRM. At the end of the day, all web products became mobile-first. The same is happening in the agentic era.

I will keep using my Google Drive and WhatsApp as long as it works for my agent. 

Of course, these products need to step up and build agent-first APIs. For instance, it's very annoying that the WhatsApp CLI doesn't support replying to a message in a thread or other basic functionalities, but I'm confident they will get it done.
